coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent graphene oxide porous particles in solution under environmental conditions produced by hydrothermal treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Rivera Tito, Harry Anderson
Hernandez-Sosa, Gerardo
Cucinotta, Fabio
Huang, Xiaokun
Quintana Caceda, Maria Esther
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescent graphene oxide particles (PGOPs) were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment. The relative transmission spectra of the PGOPs in the infrared region between 800 cm−1 and 4000 cm−1 showed frequencies that characterize chemical species such as ethers (CO), ketones (CO), carboxylic acids (COOH), double bonds of carbon (CC) and water (H2O). The emission wavelengths were 568 nm for graphene oxide with green photoluminescence (GOGP) and 634 nm for graphene oxide with orange photoluminescence (GOOP) when excited with a light source of 375 nm. Moreover, the detection of light emission at 600 nm in the photoluminescent excitation (PLE) spectrum is detected with more intensity in GOGP than GOOP due to the presence of groups CC and carboxylates ions (COO−) linked to graphene (GN) and the photon effective emission time according to the decay profile for each of them was 9.65 ns and 10.85 ns respectively. PGOPs films made by drop casting at room conditions were observed by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and Scanning Electronic Microscopy (SEM). These films acquire a wrinkled and porous shapes with a pore diameter of 51 nm and roughness (Ra) of Chemically Modified Graphene (CMG) sheets equal to 0.14 nm for GOOP and 0.21 nm for GOGP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescent graphene oxide particles
Hydrothermal treatment
Photoluminescent excitation
Photon effective emission time
Flat and porous forms from chemically modified graphene sheets

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the aqueous PGOPs solutions
Chemical and physical characterization of GOOP and GOGP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Council for Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: H. A. R. T. acknowledges the financial support National Council for Science and Technology , Perú ( 237-2015-FONDECYT ) and its economic support that this institution gave us for to make a research stay in Karlsruhe Institute of Technology through the contract N° 043-2018-FONDECYT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the preparation route to PGOPs, from GO powder obtained through the Hummers & Offeman’s modified method (1–2). The GO powder was diluted in H2O2 and water (3) to obtain a disperse GO solution (4) which was placed in a pressurized vessel with a built-in thermostat (5–6). Then the solution was extracted and taken to a dialysis bag (7). At the end of this process the dark GO solution (8) is filtered showing photoluminescence under UV light (9).
UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of PGOPs solutions obtained after the dialysis process.

The chemical reaction mechanism for carbon atoms in CMG and the formation of CO and CC groups through the radicals ·OH (a). IR spectrophotometry of GOOP (orange line) and GOGP (green line) solutions (b). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Excited state lifetimes for GOOP (orange squares) and for GOGP (green squares), recorded at λex = 475 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Photoluminescent excitation (PLE) spectra of GOOP and GOGP, recorded at λem = 600 nm.

AFM images about SiO2 substrates coated with GOOP (a) and GOGP (c) solutions. (b) and (d) show the Ra profiles for GOOP and GOGP material where L1 and L2 are the lines traced on top each sample to know how change the texture, waviness and roughness of the CMG in each sample.

Images of SiO2/GOOP (a) and SiO2/GOGP (c) shows the topography of CMG sheets supported on top SiO2, herein the blue lines traced on top of them shows the smallest graphene oxide flakes which profiles are showed in the frame (b) and (d). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
SEM images show the wrinkles of CMG sheets on GOOP film (a) in addition to the layer stacking of CMG sheets (b).
SEM Images of porous surface of GOGP material in solid state (a). The graphene sheets interlaced between them originate nanopores with a pore diameter average equal to 51 ± 1 nm (b).
Raman spectroscopy of dry PGOPs where the intensity of the D-band is greater than the intensity of the G-band in both spectra.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent graphene oxide porous particles in solution under environmental conditions produced by hydrothermal treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Braunschweig University of Technology, Pockelsstraße 14, 38106, Braunschweig, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Braunschweig University of Technology, Pockelsstraße 14, 38106 Braunschweig, Germany.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescent graphene oxide particles (PGOPs) were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment. The relative transmission spectra of the PGOPs in the infrared region between 800 cm−1 and 4000 cm−1 showed frequencies that characterize chemical species such as ethers (CO), ketones (CO), carboxylic acids (COOH), double bonds of carbon (CC) and water (H2O). The emission wavelengths were 568 nm for graphene oxide with green photoluminescence (GOGP) and 634 nm for graphene oxide with orange photoluminescence (GOOP) when excited with a light source of 375 nm. Moreover, the detection of light emission at 600 nm in the photoluminescent excitation (PLE) spectrum is detected with more intensity in GOGP than GOOP due to the presence of groups CC and carboxylates ions (COO−) linked to graphene (GN) and the photon effective emission time according to the decay profile for each of them was 9.65 ns and 10.85 ns respectively. PGOPs films made by drop casting at room conditions were observed by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and Scanning Electronic Microscopy (SEM). These films acquire a wrinkled and porous shapes with a pore diameter of 51 nm and roughness (Ra) of Chemically Modified Graphene (CMG) sheets equal to 0.14 nm for GOOP and 0.21 nm for GOGP.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Flat and porous forms from chemically modified graphene sheets

ce:section.ce:para:
In recent years photoluminescent materials made from carbon have attracted attention in the field of medicine and materials [1–3] because they offer the alternative of replacing inorganic photoluminescent materials made from toxic compounds such as cadmium [4,5]. On the other hand, the constant development of new technologies based on different allotropes of carbon with the aim of promoting them as a sustainable, ecological and cheap solution [6] has led to graphite continuing to attract attention in the field of optoelectronics. Since the discovery of its good electrical and mechanical properties and improving its optical properties by mechanically separating the transparent layers that form them, which is known as GN a 2D structural arrangement material discovered by Geim and Novoselov [7]. This brought with it the manufacture of new flexible, lightweight, small devices leading GN to position itself at the top of the most versatile materials in the field of nanotechnology. However, limitations for its large-scale production brought with it new manufacturing pathways (top-down and bottom-up) [8]. Among the best-known ways for GN production are the mechanical exfoliation technique (top-down) and the chemical vapor deposition (CVD) technique (bottom-up) where the precursor, methylene gas (H2C = CH2), is transported to a copper substrate at 1000 °C. This last technique is still expensive and the constant interest to take advantage of the chemical and physical properties of GN led to graphite to be used in a series of experiments of extreme oxidation [9,10] and hydroxylation [11] in order to separate the GN sheets from graphite through the incorporation of compounds with and without oxygen. This variety of shapes and sizes of GN raw material with different optical and electrical characteristics due to the presence of carboxyl, lactone, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups is known as graphene oxide (GO) [12,13]. This material is structurally different but chemically similar to graphite oxide [14]. From this, a variety of carbonaceous materials such as nanocomposites [15,16], chemical and thermal sensors [8,17,18], origami [19], battery electrodes [20–22], flexible supercapacitors [23,24] and others have been reported bringing with them an eco-friendly and sustainable alternative for the development of new components for electronic, biomedical [25] and sensing devices. In this context, the synthesis of photoluminescent carbonaceous materials from GO [26] has also been reported. Recently Morales-Narváez et al. reported the features and advantages of biosensor systems based on colloidal graphite oxide, which show photoluminescence. Exploiting their physicochemical characteristics which are strongly altered with the embedding of the oxygen functional groups, number of layers and the lateral size of the leave of chemically modified graphene to create interesting biosensors of simple platforms which enables develop a biosensing strategy amenable to multiplexed detection, miniaturization, and integration via lab-on-a-chip devices [27]. The photoluminescence (PL) property in GN is obtained by modifying and controlling the optical band gap (Eg) from the typical zero value in pure graphene, since the prohibited bandwidth can be adjusted by the size, shape and fraction of the sp2 domains in the sp3 matrix through the incorporation of functional groups into the hexagonal crystalline structure of GN [28]. In the same way, it has been reported that GN presents PL during a thermalization and non-thermalization process with hot electrons, calculated by the radiant flux spectrum (F (ħω)) represented by the following thermal emission model [29]:
(1)
F
ħ ω
= τ
e m E ( ħ ω )
ω 3
2
π 2
c 2
/
[ e x p
(
ħ ω
K B
T
e m
) - 1 ]
Where F (ħω) is the emissivity, τem the effective emission time for each excitation pulse, Tem the effective emission temperature and
K B
is the Boltzmann constant. On the other hand, the Eg in GO can be calculated from the UV–vis spectrum using Planck's law or Tauc's equation [30] given by:
(2)
ω 2
ε =
( h ω -
E g
)
2
Where ε is the intensity of absorption and ω (2π/λ) is the angular frequency of the incident radiation. Eq. 2 has been used to analyze the variation of the GO Eg during the time of elimination of the functional groups (phenol, carbonyl, epoxy, and tertiary alcohol).

Regardless, procedures for obtaining PGOPs involve the use of advanced processes such as pyrolysis, in which an inert atmosphere is needed in addition to the incorporation of surfactants as polyethyleneimine (PEI), poly (ethylene glycol) and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP). The surfactants have already been employed as coating materials to improve PL properties of photoluminescent carbon particles [1,2]. The main goal of these polymers is to protect the inner original nano-GN core and have a passivation effect to make it more stable. At the same time, coating materials have a potential to tune PL in nano-GN sheets to implement multiple color syntheses. On the other hands, porous materials are widely used in the fields of catalysis, electronics, gas adsorption and environmental sensing. Have been used a varieties of carbon resources like sucrose to obtain micro and mesoporous carbonaceous materials [31] using silica or zeolite Y as a template [32]. At the end of the process the template containing the polymer is lead to a calcination process of 500–1000 °C in an inert atmosphere, demanding high energy consumption.

In this work we report the synthesis of PGOPs through hydrothermal reaction of GO sheets with H2O2 and the luminescence study of the materials which show PL emission peaks at 568 nm and 634 nm upon light excitation at 375 nm, with excited state lifetimes of 9.65 ns and 10.85 ns respectively. In addition, this method allowed us to obtain a carbonaceous material with a pore diameter of 51 nm without the need to use a mold, calcination and inert atmosphere. Therefore, in this manuscript we are giving more detail on what happens to photoluminescent graphene oxide particles during their synthesis process at different temperatures and the change of their structure when they are dried under normal environmental conditions. These observations have not been reported in other articles and because graphite oxide is a very versatile material [33] it is prone to undergo different structural changes, the results do not necessarily have to be much better than those reported by other authors [34–37] because there are different parameters that limit them, which can get the material obtained to present different advantages not reported or mentioned in the literature. Therefore, through this study it has been possible to parameterize the synthesis of a photoluminescent and porous material in our laboratory; which we felt necessary to report, because the process to obtain it is cheap, and it does not use solvents that contaminate the environment [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the aqueous PGOPs solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The GO was taken to a hydrothermal process to increase the temperature and pressure of the chemical reaction that will favor the breaking of the single and double bonds of carbon atoms [39]. The scheme of the process for obtaining PGOPs is shown in Fig. 1 . The GO powder was obtained by the modified method of Hummers & Offeman [14,40]. The process outlined in Fig. 1 consisted of spreading 1 mL of GO [20 g/L] on a silicon oxide substrate (SiO2/Si, 300 nm) and then drying at 45 °C for 5 h. The dry GO material was diluted in 20 mL of ultra-pure water to obtain a solution with a concentration of 1 g/L. On the other hand, the new GO solution was poured into a beaker and 5 mL of H2O2 (Sigma Aldrich, 13%) was added to take the GO to extreme oxidation through a hydrothermal process. This process was carried out in a bottle of Teflon (polytetrafluoroethylene) of 98.19 cm3 of volume (Peruvian company Ramos), which was placed in a pressurized vessel of stainless steel; in addition, this system had incorporated a thermostat and a timer, connected to an electronic screen, to vary and control the parameters of temperature and time of duration of the process. Several experiments were carried out to obtain the desired results: the solutions of GO with H2O2 taken to the hydrothermal treatment in a temperature range of 138–171 °C for a time of 10 min show an orange light emission (GOOP) when illuminated with a violet light emitting diode (LED), while the GO taken to a hydrothermal treatment in a temperature range of 156–190 °C for a time of 32 min shows a green light emission (GOGP). In order to eliminate the excess of H2O2 in the PGOPs photoluminescent solution, a solution of manganese dioxide (MnO2) was prepared and the PGOPs were transferred to dialysis bag (Pur-A-Lyzer Mega 3500 Dialysis Kit, Sigma Aldrich). This was submerged in to MnO2 solution achieving the reduction of H2O2 given as secondary products H2O and O2 outside to the bag. On the last process, the PGOPs solutions inside the bags were filtered using PVDF microfilters of 45 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical and physical characterization of GOOP and GOGP
ce:section.ce:para: In order to detect the frequencies of the different functional groups linked to the carbon atoms, an FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific model Nicolet iS10) was used, followed by Raman characterization using a microscope with a 20x objective (Nikon Eclipse TE 2000-U), an excitation source provided by a HeNe laser of 633 nm with a power of 5.3 mW and a detection system consisting of a spectrometer (monochromatic: Horiba, Triax 320 and CCD camera: Jobin Yvon Symphony LN2). On the other hand, the morphology and topography of GOOP and GOGP were characterized using a ZEISS scanning electron microscope model Cross Beam Auriga and a DME dual scope DS 95-50E atomic force microscope. The images obtained through the microscopy measurements were analyzed using Gwyddion software.

The PGOPs absorbance was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer model USB 4000 brand Ocean Optics, equipped with two lamps (a deuterium and a halogen DH-200-BAL brand - Ocean Optics), which produce electromagnetic waves ranging from 210 nm to 800 nm. Also, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra and the decay profiles were obtained through an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer, equipped with a 450 W Xenon arc lamp, Czerny-Turner excitation and emission monochromators (1.8 nm/mm dispersion; 1800 grooves/mm), time-correlated single photon counting module and a Hamamatsu R928 P photomultiplier tube as detector (in fan assisted TE cooled housing, operating temperature −20 °C). For the decay lifetime measurements, samples were excited with an EPL-475 diode laser (471.8 nm, 61.1 ps pulse width) and data analysis was performed with numerical data reconvolution based on Marquardt-Levenberg algorithm.

Optical properties of GOOP and GOGP were measured to reveal the effect of surface chemistry on the PL behavior. PGOPs solutions obtained after hydrothermal treatment show different absorption properties: the maximum peak absorption for GOOP is given at λ = 234 nm and the spectrum also shows a less intense absorption band at λ = 303 nm. The two bands are assigned, respectively, to a π → π* and a n → π* transition. The absorption spectrum changes for the GOGP solution, as shown in Fig. 2 , where the π → π* absorption transition falls at λ = 226 nm. The photon energy (localized energy packets) can be quantified with Planck's law E = h ν where h is the Planck constant (6.63 × 10−34 J · s) and νis the frequency or can also be quantified using Eq. (2). In the same way, the wavelength of the photons is related to ν= c/λ where c is the speed of light (300 000 Km/s). If we replace ν in the Planck equation E = h c/λ the equation would be in function of λ. Therefore, the maximum E absorption values for GOOP are 5.2 eV for E234 nm and 4.1 eV for E303 nm while for GOGP the E226 nm is 5.5 eV. GO is typically regarded to as an insulator and can become a semiconductor by controlling the ratio of the sp2 and sp3 fractions of the carbon atoms bound to its structure [41], formed during the extreme oxidation of graphite when reacting with dimanganese heptoxide (Mn2O7) [12] in a non-stoichiometric way, varying the band gap of GO. However, this material, when excited with a short wavelength, does not present any luminescence phenomenon because the energy supplied by the light source is not sufficient to form electron-hole (e–h) pairs localized within small sp2 carbon clusters embedded within a sp3 matrix [42].

Nonetheless, the GOGP shows a λmax. light emission ≈568 nm. This is due to the different emitting sites of the aromatic structure of GO which includes the formation of C = OH+ radicals from the protonation of CO come from formaldehyde (H−CHO). It has been reported that protonation of the CO group in GO materials affects the HOMO – LUMO gap because the n → π* transition may no longer take place and therefore only π → π* transitions are observed [28]. On the other hand, it is well known GN pristine does not emit PL since its Eg = 0. But it can be done thermally, emitting PL from the UV to the visible region through of incident laser pulses of 30 fs (flow pump) on the GN sheets, this can be calculated using equation 1. Therefore, the GO dependence on the PL with the flow pump rules out the possibility of hot luminescence due to a photon absorption process. There are also specific properties of ultra-fast optical dynamics and non-linear optical properties (NLO) that characterize PGOPs, which should be useful for potential applications in optoelectronic devices. Thus, there are two NLO regimes (sp2 and sp3 domains) in GO with different ultra-fast optical dynamics. Accordingly, for the sp2 domains, the absorption of two photons dominates the nonlinear absorption on picosecond pulses. On the other hand, for nanosecond pulses, absorption in the excited state also influences the nonlinear response in the sp3 domains [46]. Thus, the effective photon emission time for PGOPs (Fig. 4 ) after heat treatment were analyzed by the decay profile giving a value of 10.85 ns for the GOOP when excited with a λ = 475 nm emitting light with λ = 600 nm while for the GOGP the τ was 9.65 ns emitting light with λ = 550 nm, also relating these signals to the pH of each PGOPs solution. The GOOP solution has a pH of 5.2 and the GOGP solution has a pH of 6.4. We speculate that both values influence the lifetime of the photon emission since the lower pH there is a longer emission time relating this value with the fluorophores containing the lower number of oxygenated functional groups (CO). These fluorophores that emit through the transition π - π* reside mainly inside the CMG sheets. However, the fluorophores for the transition of the green band are found at the edge of the leaves attributing it to the surface defects [47].

The PLE for both materials were measured at an emission wavelength of 600 nm (see Fig. 5 ). Both GOGP and GOOP exhibit a maximum PLE at 337 nm; however, a weaker signal appears around 500 nm, which we ascribe to the effect of the presence of CO and COOH groups at the edge of the small CMG sheets. According to the PLE spectrum the GOGP emit a photon of λ = 600 nm to be excited with a source of λ≈ 340 nm although it can emit a photon with the same emission value but with less intensity to be excited with a source of λ≈ 500 nm. This due to the different levels of energy generated by the ions in the CMG (for example, GN−COO−). To corroborate this effect was set up an optical experiment making incident a beam of laser light (λ = 532 nm ± 10) (image of Fig. 5) in the GOGP. The beam of laser light changes its colour to interact with particles in solution. This effect is related to the protonation of excited states (GN−COO-)* to (GN−COOH)* and CC. While in GOOP does not happen the same effect, it can only emit a photon of λ = 600 nm when is excited with λ = 340 nm showing a peak width from 480 to 546 nm. This effect has not been seen in GOOP probably due to the presence of CO and CO groups as shows in FTIR spectrum.

Szabó et al. [48] reported that during the graphite oxidation process it incorporates oxygenated functional groups into its structure and the evolution of the C/O ratio varies according to the GO colour after oxidation. For example, the C/O ratio for grey GO is 2.60, for brown 2.04, for orange 2.17 and for gold 2.22. In addition to causing these surface chemical variations in GO, these oxygenated functional groups cause the morphological structure of the chemically modified graphite leaf to take the form of a corrugated carbon network. On the other hand, Pal [28,29] has reported that GO presents a very weak photoluminescence in aqueous solution emitting a photon with λ = 525 nm under UV (350 nm). Likewise, the photoluminescence in GO can change when subjected to a hydrothermal treatment, Hu et al. [49] studied the variations of the atomic ratios of O/C subjecting GO to a hydrothermal treatment with ammonia, in order to synthesize GO photoluminescent nano particles finding for GO, reduced graphite oxide and N-GOQs (nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots) the values of 39.22%, 12.09% and 62.23% respectively, thus demonstrating that by leading GO to extreme oxidation the optical properties of GO can change.

PGOPs are heterogeneous materials, which lead us to analyze their morphological properties under normal environmental conditions. The AFM microscopy results (Fig. 6 a and c) show the topography of PGOPs after they dried and these form up flat and porous structures with CMG sheets of different thicknesses and shapes. The PGOPs deposited on top of SiO2 present an average roughness (Ra) of 0.14 nm to GOOP and while GOGP of 0.21 nm. They have different textures in which the valleys excel as a result of the wrinkles and folds of sheets. In the same way, after dry the film topographies show grains product of the stacking between them. The small segment selected to analysis the GOOP texture has agglomerations of layers at one end that decrease as the AFM tip is displaced (Fig. 6b). However, stacking does not have a lot influence on roughness. Meanwhile, GOGP surface contains more pores and it has less stacking keeping its roughness value nearly constant is maintained (Fig. 6 d).

The surface morphology of PGOPs dry sheets was investigated by AFM (Fig. 7 ). AFM results reveal that GOOP sheets have an average lateral size around 1 nm–4 nm (Fig. 7 a) that is near to the lateral size of one to four GO nanosheets and a width of 54 nm while CMG sheets founded in GOGP have an average thickness of 4 nm and a minimum width of 39 nm (Fig. 7 c). This shows leaves with different topography due to the functional groups anchored to GN leaves at its edges. These clusters of CMG were analyzed using Gwyddion and Molinspiration software, this last was used to simulate the change of the GN sheet thought incorporation of functional groups like carbonyl, carboxyl acid and ketones (Fig. 7 b and d).

These parameters are also related to the effect of PL on GOOP and GOGP since the leaves and the stacking among themselves after being dried give an idea they are kept separated in solution by loads or connected by hydrogen bridge links (CMG-OH-H-O-H-OH-OH-C-CMG). But when these molecules are lost the CMG leaves undergo a chaotic rearrangement leading the formation of a nearly flat material (its present wrinkles) for GOOP (Fig. 8 a) and porous for GOGP (Fig. 9 a).

The average pore size was measured through a logarithmic normal distribution giving as a result 51 ± 1 nm (Fig. 9 a). Both materials show a different topography when dried. In addition, in Fig. 9b The CMG sheets intertwined between them form nanofibers with a width ≈ 8 nm. The opposite occurs with the GOOP, which shows CMG leaves wrinkled which are supported by the agglomerations which are assembled in the drying process (Fig. 8b). Despite this, according to the optical experiments with GO carried out by Inhwa et al., [50] the water molecules detachment from the interlinear layers affects the optical and morphological properties of material varying its effective refractive index (n) and absorption coefficient (k) as a function of λ. The assumed values n and k are 2 and 0 respectively for GO. However, GOGP leaf defects are derived from defective states associated with oxygenated functional groups originating from vacancies, interstitial atoms, and various functional groups surrounding the edges of CMG leaves which are involved with the PL.

The average defect distances (LD) contents in CMG sheets have been calculated using equation 3 [51].
(3)
L D 2
(
n m
2
)
=
( 4.3
± 1.3 )
x
10 3
E L 4
(
e V
4
)
[
I D
I G
]
- 1
Where EL = ℏωL is the photon energy of the incident laser in eV, for the experiment a laser of 633 nm (1.96 eV) was used and ωL is the excitation frequency. Raman microscopy is the most versatile tool for the characterization of carbon allotropes. For the analysis two signal characteristics were used that characterize the defective GN in the Raman spectrum: defects related to mode D (∼1350 cm−1) and mode G (∼1600 cm−1) (Fig. 10 ). Peak D only occurs in GN with Raman-active defects so that the intensity of the mode D and G (ID/IG) peak ratio [52] is a measure of the density of the defects [53]. The defect density (nD) in the CMG sheets can be estimated using equation 4.
(4)
n D
(
c m
- 2
) = ( 7.3 ± 2.2 )
x
10 9
x
E L 4
(
I D
I G
)

The LD results for GOOP and GOGP dry were 16.62 nm and 16.80 nm respectively while for nD the calculated values were equal to 1.12 cm2 and 1.10 cm2 for each material. The LD values are > 10 nm which means that total area contributing to the dispersion of D-band is proportional to the number of point defects [54]. Therefore, the CMG sheets LD in GOGP has more point defects than GOOP CMG sheets. The points defects contribute to the emergence of fibers during the drying process by creating voids in the material. Moreover, the nD values to porous material are lower than flat material due to the functional groups location or punctual charges in the CMG network, which make possible for both materials to show different shapes.

We conclude, the PGOPs obtained through hydrothermal treatment present PL due to the formation of oxygenated functional groups in the oxidized graphite structure. This is also related to the thickness of the dispersed sheets in aqueous solution and both factors are affected when are dried. In addition, the ions formed by the ·OH radicals generated during the oxidation process by the temperature increase in the reactor influences in the light color emission of the PGOPs, which is directly involved with the Eg of CMG sheets. Achieving tuning the PL without needed to use polymers. On the other hand, the defects in the carbon network of the CMG sheets cause the stacked of them under environmental conditions while they dry, which leads to the formation of pores with an average size of 51 ± 1 nm without the need for high temperatures.

H. A. R. T. acknowledges the financial support National Council for Science and Technology, Perú (237-2015-FONDECYT) and its economic support that this institution gave us for to make a research stay in Karlsruhe Institute of Technology through the contract N° 043-2018-FONDECYT.
The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest related to this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H. A. R. T. acknowledges the Thin Films Laboratory of the National University of Engineering for all the facilities to carry out the experiments and Dr. Anne Habermehl for her help in Raman measurements.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, yellow, and blue luminescence by graphene quantum dots: syntheses, mechanism, and cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gao T, Wang X, Yang LY, He H, Ba XX, Zhao J, Jiang FL, Liu Y. Red, yellow, and blue luminescence by graphene quantum dots: syntheses, mechanism, and cellular imaging. ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 2017; 9(29):24846–56.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Super-cationic carbon quantum dots synthesized from spermidine as an eye drop formulation for topical treatment of bacterial keratitis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jian HJ, Wu RS, Lin TY, Li YJ, Lin HJ, Harroun SG, Lai JY, Huang CC. Super-cationic carbon quantum dots synthesized from spermidine as an eye drop formulation for topical treatment of bacterial keratitis. ACS Nano 2017; 11(7):6703–16.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Science and technology roadmap for graphene, related two-dimensional crystals, and hybrid systems
